class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Online Learning: Theory and Evidence
learning_objectives: Summarize the research base on online learning effectiveness
Distinguish between fully online, blended, and web-enhanced delivery models
Apply the equivalency framework to online course design
readings: Means, B. et al. - Evaluation of Evidence-Based Practices in Online Learning (DOE meta-analysis)
Moore, M. - Theory of Transactional Distance

lecture_notes.introduction: Online learning is no longer an experiment. Decades of research show it can be as effective as face-to-face instruction when well designed. This session examines what the evidence actually says and what conditions must be met.
key_concepts: DOE meta-analysis findings: blended > online ≥ face-to-face
Transactional distance theory: dialogue, structure, learner autonomy
No significant difference phenomenon
Quality indicators: interaction, feedback, assessment, support
Myths vs. evidence in online learning discourse
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evidence evaluation exercise: read three claims about online learning and trace each to the underlying research, evaluating whether the claim is supported

review.value: Students in online conditions performed modestly better than face-to-face; blended conditions showed the largest advantage; the key variable is instructional design quality, not the medium
Moore's theory: as physical distance increases, psychological distance (transactional distance) must be reduced through increased dialogue and appropriate structure

learning_objectives: Design self-paced learning modules with clear structure and pacing
Create asynchronous activities that promote deep learning
Apply chunking and sequencing strategies to online content
readings: Vai, M. & Sosulski, K. - Essentials of Online Course Design Ch. 3-5
Darby, F. & Lang, J. - Small Teaching Online Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Asynchronous learning gives students flexibility but requires exceptional design to maintain engagement and prevent isolation. The challenge is creating structured experiences that guide self-directed learning without becoming rigid.
key_concepts: Module structure: overview, objectives, content, activity, assessment, summary
Chunking: breaking content into 10-15 minute segments
Pacing mechanisms: weekly schedules, deadlines, progress indicators
Self-assessment and reflection as engagement tools
Preventing 'content dump' courses: activity-centered design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Module blueprint exercise: design the structure for one week of an asynchronous course, specifying content chunks, activities, and pacing mechanisms

review.value: Instead of organizing around content delivery (read this, watch this), organize around what students DO: analyze a case, debate a position, create an artifact, solve a problem
Fully self-paced courses have lower completion rates; regular deadlines with some flexibility (e.g., two attempts, lowest quiz dropped) balance structure and autonomy

learning_objectives: Facilitate engaging synchronous sessions using video conferencing tools
Apply active learning techniques adapted for synchronous online environments
Design backup plans for common technical failure scenarios
readings: Lemov, D. - Teaching in the Online Classroom Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Synchronous online sessions are not Zoom lectures. The same active learning principles that work in classrooms can work online, but the techniques must be adapted for the medium. A 50-minute talking-head session will lose students within 10 minutes.
key_concepts: Breakout rooms: design, timing, and accountability
Polling and backchannel as participation tools
Chat waterfall and think-pair-share adaptations
Managing the hybrid challenge: some in-person, some remote
Technical contingency planning: what happens when Zoom fails?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live synchronous facilitation practice: each student facilitates a 5-minute segment using at least two active learning techniques
setup_requirements: Zoom or Teams with breakout room capability

review.value: Change the activity every 10 minutes maximum; alternate between listening, doing, and discussing to maintain engagement in synchronous online sessions
Provide written instructions (not just verbal), assign roles, set a timer visible to all, and require a tangible deliverable (shared doc, posted answer) to ensure accountability

learning_objectives: Design strategies for establishing social presence in online courses
Create collaborative learning activities that build community
Address isolation and belonging challenges in online learning
readings: Garrison, D.R. - E-Learning in the 21st Century Ch. 4
Brewer, S. & Klein, J. - Types of Positive Interdependence and Their Relationship to Individual Student Achievement

lecture_notes.introduction: The biggest challenge in online learning is not content delivery but human connection. Students who feel isolated drop out. Students who feel part of a learning community persist, engage more deeply, and learn more.
key_concepts: Social presence: the degree to which learners feel socially and emotionally connected
Ice breakers that actually work online: purpose-driven introductions
Collaborative learning structures: jigsaw, case-based discussion, peer review
Instructor social presence: tone, responsiveness, video presence
Addressing loneliness and motivation challenges in online settings
lecture_notes.demonstration: Community building design challenge: design a first-week sequence for an online course that establishes social presence without feeling forced or artificial
setup_requirements: Examples of effective and ineffective icebreakers

review.value: Garrison defines it as the ability to project personal characteristics into the community, making participants feel real and present. It correlates with satisfaction and perceived learning.
Collaborative activities require genuine interdependence (each person's contribution is necessary) — not just 'group work' where one person does everything

learning_objectives: Compare major e-learning authoring tools by features and use cases
Explain SCORM, xAPI, and LTI standards and their purposes
Create a basic e-learning module using an authoring tool
readings: Comparative review of Articulate Rise, Adobe Captivate, and H5P

lecture_notes.introduction: E-learning authoring tools allow non-programmers to create interactive digital learning content. Standards like SCORM and xAPI ensure that content can be tracked across platforms. Understanding both tools and standards is essential for the educational technologist.
key_concepts: Authoring tool categories: slide-based, screen-recorded, branching, responsive
Articulate Storyline/Rise, Adobe Captivate, H5P, iSpring
SCORM: legacy standard for content packaging and basic tracking
xAPI (Tin Can): modern standard for tracking any learning activity
LTI: connecting external tools to an LMS securely
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rapid e-learning build: create a 5-screen interactive lesson using Articulate Rise or H5P within the session, including a knowledge check with feedback
setup_requirements: Articulate Rise trial account or H5P access
Pre-prepared content outline for rapid build
LMS sandbox for testing SCORM packages

review.value: SCORM tracks completion and score within a browser-based LMS; xAPI tracks any learning activity (mobile, VR, on-the-job, social) in a Learning Record Store, enabling richer analytics
Learning Tools Interoperability allows external tools (e.g., a simulation, a publisher platform) to integrate seamlessly with an LMS, passing grades and user data securely

learning_objectives: Apply Quality Matters standards to evaluate online course design
Conduct a structured peer review of an online course
Develop a continuous improvement plan based on quality review findings
readings: Legon, R. & Garrett, R. - The Changing Landscape of Online Education (CHLOE report)

lecture_notes.introduction: How do you know if an online course is well designed? Quality Matters provides the most widely adopted peer review process for online course design. This session equips you to both evaluate courses and defend your own design decisions against quality standards.
key_concepts: Quality Matters: 8 general standards, 42 specific review standards
Alignment as the core QM principle
Peer review process: trained reviewers, evidence-based ratings
OSCQR (SUNY) as an alternative rubric
Continuous improvement: using review data to iterate course design
lecture_notes.demonstration: QM mini-review: use a simplified QM rubric to review a sample online course module and produce a summary of strengths and recommendations
setup_requirements: Sample online course module for review

review.value: The most critical QM standard: learning objectives, assessment, instructional materials, and activities must all align — a course can look beautiful but fail QM if alignment is absent
Quality review is not a one-time certification; it is part of a cycle: design → implement → collect data → review → revise → re-implement

quizzes.topic: Online Learning Foundations and Asynchronous Design

questions.question: Moore's theory of transactional distance identifies three variables that affect the psychological distance between instructor and learner. Name all three and explain how an online course designer can manipulate one of them to reduce transactional distance.
questions.answer: The three variables are dialogue (the quality and quantity of communication), structure (the rigidity or flexibility of course design), and learner autonomy (the learner's ability to self-direct). A designer can increase dialogue by incorporating regular instructor video updates, prompt discussion facilitation with substantive feedback, and optional office hours. More dialogue reduces transactional distance because it makes the instructor feel present and responsive.

rubric.key: Selects one and provides specific design strategies
Explains the mechanism by which transactional distance is reduced

questions.question: What is 'content dump' course design, and why does it produce poor learning outcomes? Describe an alternative design approach.
questions.answer: Content dump design is organizing an online course as a repository of readings, slides, and videos that students passively consume without structured activities or interaction. It produces poor outcomes because passive consumption does not engage the generative processing needed for meaningful learning. Alternative: activity-centered design, where each module is organized around what students DO (analyze, create, debate, solve) rather than what they READ or WATCH. Content is provided in service of activities, not as an end in itself.

rubric.key: Accurate description of content dump design
Explains why it fails using learning science
Describes a specific alternative with examples

quizzes.topic: Community, Authoring Tools, and Quality Assurance

questions.question: Explain the difference between SCORM and xAPI. Give one example of a learning activity that xAPI can track but SCORM cannot.
questions.answer: SCORM (Sharable Content Object Reference Model) tracks basic interactions within browser-based LMS content: completion, score, time spent, and simple interactions. xAPI (Experience API) tracks any learning activity from any context using 'actor-verb-object' statements stored in a Learning Record Store. Example: xAPI can track a nursing student practicing CPR on a physical mannequin in a simulation lab ('Jane practiced CPR on mannequin simulator'), or reading a research article on a mobile app during a commute. SCORM cannot track these because they occur outside a browser-based LMS.

rubric.key: Accurate comparison of SCORM and xAPI capabilities
Specific example of an xAPI-only trackable activity
Explains why SCORM cannot track the example

questions.question: What is the core alignment principle in Quality Matters? A course has excellent video production, beautiful visual design, and engaging discussion activities, but objectives, assessments, and activities target different cognitive levels. Would this course pass a QM review? Explain.
questions.answer: The core QM alignment principle requires that learning objectives, assessments, instructional materials, and learning activities all target the same knowledge and cognitive level. The described course would NOT pass QM review despite its high production quality. If objectives target 'analyze' but assessments test 'remember' and activities require 'create,' there is a fundamental alignment failure. Students may complete engaging activities without being assessed on what they practiced, and may be tested on content they never actively engaged with. QM treats alignment as essential, not cosmetic.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the course would fail QM review
Explains why misalignment undermines learning despite high production quality

major_assignments.title: Online Course Module Design and Development
major_assignments.description: Design and develop a complete 3-week online course module using an LMS or authoring tool. The module must include asynchronous content, at least one synchronous session plan, a collaborative learning activity, formative and summative assessments, and a QM self-review. Conduct a one-to-one formative evaluation with a peer learner and revise based on feedback.
objectives: Apply online course design principles to create a functional learning module
Integrate asynchronous and synchronous learning activities
Conduct formative evaluation and revise based on data
deliverables: Functional 3-week online module in LMS or authoring tool
QM self-review checklist with evidence annotations
Formative evaluation report (observations from peer learner tryout)
Revision summary documenting changes made based on evaluation

criteria: Clear module structure with consistent navigation
Content chunked appropriately with active learning embedded
Pacing mechanisms and learner support features
Active learning techniques adapted for online synchronous delivery
Clear facilitation plan with timing and contingencies
Collaborative activity with genuine positive interdependence
Social presence strategies for instructor and learners
QM self-review demonstrates alignment across components
Formative evaluation conducted with a real learner

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on online learning theory and design principles

participation.description: Online discussions, synchronous session participation, and peer reviews

major_assignments.description: Online course module design and development project

midterm_examination.description: Covers online learning research, asynchronous/synchronous design, and community building

final_project.description: Complete online course with quality review, pilot testing, and revision

course_policies.attendance: This course is delivered in a blended format. In-person sessions and synchronous online sessions both require attendance. More than 2 absences will affect the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Module development has staggered milestones that must be met for peer review.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review and collaborative design critique are integral to this course. Final deliverables must be individually produced.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All course content must be original or properly attributed. Use of AI-generated content in course modules must be disclosed and justified. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Reliable internet, LMS access, video conferencing capability (webcam and microphone), and access to at least one e-learning authoring tool.
